title: Meeting Rooms II
code: var endTimes = new PriorityQueue<int, int>();  // min-heap

frames.caption: Empty input? No. Sort by start (already sorted), then create an empty min-heap of end times.
frames.note: Sort by start so the timeline runs left to right; then a single min-heap keyed on end time always exposes the soonest-freeing room at its top. Sorting is the O(n log n) dominant cost; the sweep that follows does n heap pushes and up to n pops, each O(log n).

frames.caption: Process meeting [0,30] (start 0). Check the soonest-freeing room.
frames.note: Each meeting is handled in start order. We first ask whether a room has already freed up by the time this meeting begins, then we occupy a room for it. The heap top is the only end time we ever need to inspect — it is the earliest one.

frames.caption: Heap is empty — no room to reuse, this meeting needs a fresh room.
frames.note: The very first meeting always allocates a new room: there is nothing in the heap to reuse. Count goes from 0 to 1.

frames.caption: Enqueue end 30. rooms = 1 — new peak, maxRooms = 1.
frames.note: After deciding reuse-or-not, this meeting always occupies a room until its own end, so we Enqueue(meeting.end). The heap size now equals the number of rooms in use at this moment; maxRooms tracks the largest that size ever reaches. That peak — the maximum simultaneous overlap — is exactly the minimum rooms required.

frames.caption: Process meeting [5,10] (start 5). Check the soonest-freeing room.
frames.note: Each meeting is handled in start order. We first ask whether a room has already freed up by the time this meeting begins, then we occupy a room for it. The heap top is the only end time we ever need to inspect — it is the earliest one.

frames.caption: heap.Peek() = 30. Is 30 <= 5 (this start)? no.
frames.note: The soonest-freeing room ends AFTER this meeting starts (heap.Peek() > meeting.start), so every room is still busy at this instant. No Dequeue happens — this meeting must claim a brand-new room, which is what pushes the concurrency (and possibly the peak) up by one.

frames.caption: Enqueue end 10. rooms = 2 — new peak, maxRooms = 2.
frames.note: After deciding reuse-or-not, this meeting always occupies a room until its own end, so we Enqueue(meeting.end). The heap size now equals the number of rooms in use at this moment; maxRooms tracks the largest that size ever reaches. That peak — the maximum simultaneous overlap — is exactly the minimum rooms required.

frames.caption: Process meeting [15,20] (start 15). Check the soonest-freeing room.
frames.note: Each meeting is handled in start order. We first ask whether a room has already freed up by the time this meeting begins, then we occupy a room for it. The heap top is the only end time we ever need to inspect — it is the earliest one.

frames.caption: heap.Peek() = 10. Is 10 <= 15 (this start)? yes.
frames.note: The heap top is the END time of the room that frees soonest. Because meetings are processed in START order, if that earliest end is ≤ this meeting's start, the room is already empty when this meeting begins — so we Dequeue() and REUSE it instead of allocating a new one. Back-to-back meetings can share a room: the reuse test is `<=`, so a room freeing exactly at this start counts as available.

frames.caption: Dequeue 10: that room is free, reuse it. rooms drops to 1 before re-occupying.
frames.note: The heap top is the END time of the room that frees soonest. Because meetings are processed in START order, if that earliest end is ≤ this meeting's start, the room is already empty when this meeting begins — so we Dequeue() and REUSE it instead of allocating a new one. Back-to-back meetings can share a room: the reuse test is `<=`, so a room freeing exactly at this start counts as available.

frames.caption: Enqueue end 20. rooms = 2.
frames.note: After deciding reuse-or-not, this meeting always occupies a room until its own end, so we Enqueue(meeting.end). The heap size now equals the number of rooms in use at this moment; maxRooms tracks the largest that size ever reaches. That peak — the maximum simultaneous overlap — is exactly the minimum rooms required.

frames.caption: Loop ends. Heap holds 2 end times — peak concurrency. Minimum rooms = 2.
frames.note: The final heap size equals the number of rooms occupied at the busiest instant, which is the maximum overlap and therefore the minimum rooms needed. On this input two meetings (0..30 and 5..10) overlap, then [15,20] reuses the room [5,10] freed at 10 — so the answer is 2. Total cost O(n log n), space O(n) for the heap.
